Kali mirch/black pepper 5-6
10-12 cloves of garlic
Coriander seeds 4-5 spoons
Saunf/fennel seeds 4-5 spoons
Jeera/cumin seeds 4-5 spoons
Red Chillies (as much you see in the video)
6-7 cloves
Fry all this in 2 tablespoons of oil until chillies get crispy..
And ground it ..can add shredded coconut while grounding this masala
Add some half fried onions (more onions more acidity)
Later marinate chicken with this masala and garnish it with coconut flakes and coriander/cilantro leaves for 2 hours
Heat 4 tablespoons of oil+ put onions, fry until red
Add chicken
Don't add water...since this is sukka (dry) masala
Heat chicken until it precipitates and cooks itself
Enjoy

Aryan I don't know why you were quick to assume I was from UP Bihar, but anyway, have you ever been to vidharbha? Solapur? Konkan? Or are you assuming Maharashtra is developed because of Mumbai-Pune-Nagpur? Major cities like Aurangabad still face water shortages... Many colonies in Aurangabad either have to order water tankers or wait for 3-4 days for potable water...is this the definition of development? Telco has been moving it's production units to other states year after year for the last ten years... increasing 'skilled' unemployment in cities like pune pcmc, is this development?
India's financial capital still doesn't have a working metro system, and if you are going to tell me that ghatkopar line is enough to suffice the needs then you would be delusional...pune has been asking for metro since 2006-7...when it's population was closing up to 4 million...now the city has passed 6 million people and we still don't have a functional metro...let me not even get started about pune ring road...half of our five star midcs are empty, why? Five star means continuous electricity, 4 lane road connectivity, rail connectivity, etc...still companies aren't flocking in to setup factories, why? Newly formed states like telengana would catch up with Maharashtra's development soon..
Saudi Aramco was planning to invest around 50 billion in Ratnagiri which has halted...(there are many reasons for this, pandemic being one) but what Saudi officials state right now is change in local government and leadership... people of kolhapur-sangli get a flood-scare nearly every odd year...
I don't want to discard or discredit what Maharashtra has done in the last 70 years but much more can be done...I don't mean to offend anyone from UP Bihar, but Maharashtra should not compare itself with underdeveloped states...we have gone passed that...compare Maharashtra to Tokyo bay area, Shanghai, California etc then you will see how developed Maharashtra is...all I want to say is first become the best then teach the rest.
There is a marathi saying: "loka sanghe brahmadnyan, swatah korde pashan" that should not happen with Maharashtra

@anant_shinde I did do a study on this during my masters and there are only about three regions in the world that have suitable wind speed and water depth along the coastline, ie norsesea, between England and Scandinavia, northeast usa, and above Taiwan....the thing about offshore wind turbine projects is that minimum turbine design is for 10MW and speeds required for that kind of power generation is 10m/s...so instead of building 200ft+ high turbines in the sea and worry about wind speed and corrosion everyday, 100ft lower capacity turbines (onshore) that require minimum 5m/s for power generation would be more beneficial

@sonasyummykitchen2027well that’s because you are dividing 2025 gdp estimates with 2011 population data. But ground reality is different. 2011 population estimate is 1.5 cr. In 2025, it would be around 2cr + seasonal migrants from Bihar, Jharkhand and illegal migrants from Bangladesh. Considering all this, the per capita gdp would drop significantly.

I am not saying that other Indian cities don’t experience seasonal migrants from Bihar and illegals from Bangladesh, however Kolkata due to closer proximity has to accommodate a larger chunk.

@killme2675 I don't think nationalism is the main issue that hinders our progress, it's more to do with illiteracy within the masses, like India claims 70% literacy but probably only 10% have logical thinking, probably same for Pakistan. 2nd thing is nepotism, it has always been a huge thing in our cultures...the top 1% in politics, business (military for Pakistan) always look for stability for their kin, it doesn't matter to them if their community as a whole would suffer, that's why they are so resistant to change (they don't have 'best for the job' attitude)...if you keep the masses illiterate and impoverished, they would be constants in the ruling class...that is one reason why India chose Modi over Rahul Gandhi...they were desparate for change...and I don't think Congress would come to power until they actually try to solve India's problems. Probably the same reason why Pakistan chose Imran Khan (even though he is rich I don't think he had political connections and stuff, not sure about that, but I guess Pakistans population was tired of Bhuttos and shariffs)...and I can see change in Indian government's attitude towards its problems...they have identified many redundant British era laws that were hampering progress and promoting corruption... Bureaucracy in India has been getting more freedom to implement the right policies, The toilet program is one of that, it is an embarrassing problem but they have recognised it and started to solve it which has improved sanitation facilities; along with health care, micro loans are given to farmers and small shop vendors to start small businesses, affordable housing has helped construct at least 5 crore houses in the last 6 years of Modi gov, highways are being built at 5 times more pace and thier goal is to achieve 8 times more speed by 2024...and the best thing that this gov could have done and which they are doing is increasing transparency...they have made most forms, applications etc online, they have started to use block chain to identify and solve major issues...now I'm not trying to advertise Modi gov, I'm not going to get anything from it, but I can definitely see change, I think what both the populations should understand is that 'you can change your allies but you can't change your neighbours'...so a healthy developed Pakistan would any day be better for both india and pakistan...and if you read all of this, thank you for your patience and hope you have a great day ahead

i did a rough calculation assuming 15kg of lithium/EV and we can produce around 393 million vehicles. Not a small amount. But I guess we also have other applications of lithium in electronics and pharma. Plus the extraction cost of lithium is also a huge factor. Within the Lithium triangle in south america, there are lithium salt pans. So i guess Indian lithium is going to more expensive since we would have to include mining and other refining activities. Similar to Venezuelan oil whose extraction cost is 3 times that of Saudi oil. Venezuela can only make a profit when oil price is around $70+/barrel.

H1B has a cap of 80k/year. And it is a three year visa. So, at any given point, there would be 240k H1b visa holders. 30k of 240k is 12.5%. I will leave the discretion up to the viewers whether 12% is high or low. And from what he said, it was 30k for the year 2023. Oil discoveries are fine and all. We need to lower extraction costs for these oil fields to be a reality and actually help the Indian economy.
Guyanese, Saudi oil is extracted below $10/barrel, whereas Russia Canada @15~30 America averages at 40$ barrel…oil companies in US extract oil only when international barrel rates are above $80, that’s when they get a 100% margin which is required for refining costs and booking profits etc.

India currently extracts oil @$55~65, leaving our companies with very little margin.

The world (3rd world and 2nd world ie manufacturing cheap Labour countries and resource extracting countries) are moving away from dollar and euro dominance in trade and moving towards bilateral currency swaps. This is leading to massive dedollarisation and losing of purchasing power of western countries. Many NRIs are going to start reverse migration back to India in the next 5-10 years. Indian expats are amongst the most successful groups in these western countries, and will start facing persecution as soon as local (white) people start losing jobs and wealth. Second would definitely be medical field.
Third but not last would be sports sector.

Don't want to be a pessimist, but Solar panel (pv) manufacturing is an energy intensive task plus it takes a huge toll on the environment...plus, the Sun belt is not that suitable for solar pvs since the temperature range is on the higher side and pvs operate better under cooler climatic conditions (between 15-25°C for most efficient generation around 18-20%)... Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) is probably a suitable option since it is based on heat and we can use this tech for maximum solar power in the Sun belt, but again it would be costly and we would be exposing the environment to lot of heavy metals...India for now should invest in material sciences to find a suitable material that can give us solar energy at the highest efficiency, and can be produced locally with minimum environmental impact...pervoskite is one possible option to give efficient generation at high temperatures but again it has expensive heavy metals...but yes, this discussion is needed in India and Indian Colleges should ask their students to invest time in research rather than just obtain a degree

In India's latest national budget, motorcycles with engines over 350 CC capacity would fall under higher tax rate, so the domestic indian market is going to take some time to adjust to this. Not that there is any sudden reduction in demand for 350+ capacity bikes in India, but companies are going to hold their horses back a little when it comes to launching new platforms using their domestic brand names (at least until the spending capacity of Indian consumers normalizes again), rather they would rely on foreign brand partners or acquisitions to launch newer platforms with higher capacities with joint production and/or sale in India and for Indian export markets like Africa and South America. Since this market is still small compared to India's domestic market, i would not expect a a 600cc or 750cc mid bikes coming below the price of R130,000 at the moment, because economies of scale would not justify it. However, in the next 3-5 years, when Indian spending capacity crosses a certain threshold (say $3500 per capita gdp), you would see a whole new variety in mid size bike segments, maybe at an even lower price point.

Italy’s TFR is 1.21 and median age 48.1. So yea, every year hence forth is going to get more worse than the previous moving forward.
Other countries are not going to do any better either. France and Germany and not far off from these. Actually, the whole developed world is probably sitting at 1.5 TFR and median age of around 40-44. In the next ten years, there would be more retirees and pensioners than youthful tax payers. There goes your social security net. And if you think acquiring people from war torn countries like Syria and Afghanistan will alleviate the problem, then you are dead wrong cause there are problems with assimilation.
People think Africa, India and Latin America would be safe bets in the next two decades for investment. But how are you going to solve the dropping fertility rates in those countries? As they develop, their TFRs are dropping rapidly as well. India used to have a TFR of 2.9-3 now it’s borderline 2-2.1. As incomes increase, stress increases, people avoid having families and kids. If India doesn’t solve this problem now, we would be in Italy’s shoes in the next three four decades.
